items.q: When you push a wall, what does Newton's third law say about the wall?
items.correct: The wall exerts an equal and opposite force on you at the same instant.
items.misconception: The wall only pushes back if your force is strong enough to make it react.
items.random_wrong: The wall pushes back with a force proportional to how stiff it is, not equal to your force.
items.why: Students think action-reaction is causal and sequential — push first, then the wall 'responds' — instead of being two aspects of one interaction.

items.q: Does Hooke's law apply to a spring for any amount of stretching?
items.correct: No — it only holds up to the limit of proportionality; beyond that the relationship between force and extension is no longer linear.
items.misconception: Yes — Hooke's law applies to any spring at any extension.
items.random_wrong: Only when the force is small enough to be ignored.
items.why: Students apply F = kx universally without noting the limit of proportionality, leading to over-prediction at large extensions.

items.q: If the distance between two masses doubles, what happens to the gravitational force between them?
items.correct: It quarters — gravitational force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance.
items.misconception: It halves — gravitational force is inversely proportional to distance.
items.random_wrong: It doubles — more distance means more space for gravity to act through.
items.why: Students default to linear relationships and forget the inverse-square law; they apply 1/r reasoning instead of 1/r^2.

items.q: Higher orbital radius means orbital speed is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Lower. “Higher” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Orbit at r, period T. Same body, orbit at 4r. New period in units of T?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
